description: When configuring the same BGP neighbor under both IPv4 and IPv6 address families on a router, if a maximum prefix limit is set on one address family (like IPv4), exceeding that limit can potentially impact the BGP session for the other address family (IPv6) as well.

messages.content: For 9300S_10040 AOS-CX 10.16.1006, what caveat is documented for BGP?

Feature Caveat: BGP - When configuring the same BGP neighbor under both IPv4 and IPv6 address families on a router, if a maximum prefix limit is set on one address family (like IPv4), exceeding that limit can potentially impact the BGP session for the other address family (IPv6) as well.

description: If there is no user configuration for DNS servers and HPE Aruba Networking Central is configured, the switch defaults to using Google's DNS server at 8.8.8.8. HPE Aruba Networking Central is enabled by default across all switch platforms.

description: When a switch can connect to HPE Aruba Networking Central but is not registered in the inventory or lacks a proper license, it will repeatedly disconnect and reconnect. This cycle will continue until the switch is properly registered or a license is obtained. To avoid this unnecessary reconnection cycle, it is best practice to disable HPE Aruba Networking Central until the switch is registered or a license is obtained.

messages.content: For 9300S_10040 AOS-CX 10.16.1006, what caveat is documented for Central?

Feature Caveat: Central - When a switch can connect to HPE Aruba Networking Central but is not registered in the inventory or lacks a proper license, it will repeatedly disconnect and reconnect. This cycle will continue until the switch is properly registered or a license is obtained. To avoid this unnecessary reconnection cycle, it is best practice to disable HPE Aruba Networking Central until the switch is registered or a license is obtained.

description: Policies containing both MAC and IPv6 classes are not allowed.

Feature Caveat: Classifiers - Policies containing both MAC and IPv6 classes are not allowed.

Feature Caveat: Classifiers - For secure classifier policy modifications, HPE Aruba Networking strongly encourages a three-step process: bring down the port, make the modifications, and then bring the port back up.

description: When SNMP is enabled via the switch CLI, it can take between 1-2 minutes for the SNMP daemon to be ready to respond to requests. messages.content: For 9300S_10040 AOS-CX 10.16.1006, what caveat is documented for SNMP?

description: Symptom: Starting in 10.16.1006, the default system profile for the 9300/9300S platforms is "DC-Optimized-Leaf". DC-Optimized profiles do not support IP tunnels (GRE, 6in4, 6in6). Scenario: Starting in 10.16.1006, the default system profile for the 9300/9300S platforms is "DC-Optimized-Leaf". DC-Optimized profiles do not support IP tunnels (GRE, 6in4, 6in6). Workaround: If desired the switch profile can be manually changed back to the previous default system profile of "Leaf". This can be done with the 'profile' configuration command. Note: if IP tunnels are required, it is necessary to change out of the DC-Optimized profile.

description: Symptom: A VSF stack member switch unexpectedly reboots due to the ops-switchd process crashing with a signal: 6 error. Scenario: In some scenarios with excessive ACL event logging, a crash may occur due to a failure in the system when processing a high volume of ACL log entries. Workaround: Minimize the use of the log keyword in ACL entries, especially on high-traffic rules. Consider using ACL counters instead of logging for monitoring traffic patterns.

description: Symptom: Missing ARP entries showing as "incomplete" in the ARP table, causing connectivity issues in remote VTEP. Scenario: This issue may occur in an EVPN VXLAN environment with VSX pairs when a VIP (Virtual IP) moves from one node to another node, MAC address aging occurs or after executing "clear mac-address" on the switch. Workaround: A temporary workaround is to add a static ARP entry for the affected IP addresses.

description: Symptom: Network Management Systems does not display IPv4 addresses for Aruba CX switches running AOS-CX version 10.13. Scenario: This issue occurs when the NMS queries the switch using SNMP and relies on either of the following OIDs to retrieve IPv4 address information: 1.3.6.1.2.1.4.20.1.2(deprecated) or1.3.6.1.2.1.4.34.1.5(current but not accessible in 10.13). In AOS-CX 10.13, neither OID returns valid IPv4 data, resulting in incomplete device visibility. Workaround: N/A.

In AOS-CX 10.13, neither OID returns valid IPv4 data, resulting in incomplete device visibility. Workaround: N/A.

description: Symptom: In an L2VPN network, when a DHCP client sends an IP request with the broadcast flag unset (unicast), duplicate DHCP client packets are observed at the DHCP server-one from each VTEP. As a result, the client does not receive an IP address. Scenario: To prevent duplicate DHCP client packets from each VTEP in an L2VPN topology, it is recommended to configure "no dhcp-relay l2vpn-clients" on the switch. However, after saving the switch configuration and rebooting, the relay daemon does not correctly apply all configuration settings at boot time, even though the configuration persists in the database. This causes the duplicate packet issue to reappear. Workaround: To restore correct behavior, manually enable and then disable the L2VPN client configuration using "dhcp-relay l2vpn-clients" followed by "no dhcp-relay l2vpn-clients".

description: Symptom: Multicast traffic loss may occur on interfaces with PIM-Sparse Mode (PIM-SM) enabled in overlay networks. Affected multicast flows fail to replicate across VXLAN tunnels, resulting in service disruption. Scenario: In VSX deployments, multicast replication may fail during system boot or reload if the associated L3 interfaces for overlay VLANs are not fully initialized. This is more likely in configurations involving MCLAGs and delayed VSX synchronization, where multicast processing begins before the L3 interface is ready. Workaround: Toggle the pim-sparse configuration on the impacted interfaces.

description: Symptom: Starting in 10.16.1006, the default system profile for the 9300/9300S platforms is "DC-Optimized-Leaf". DC-Optimized profiles do not support IP tunnels (GRE, 6in4, 6in6). Scenario: Starting in 10.16.1006, the default system profile for the 9300/9300S platforms is "DC-Optimized-Leaf". DC-Optimized profiles do not support IP tunnels (GRE, 6in4, 6in6). Workaround: If desired the switch profile can be manually changed back to the previous default system profile of "Leaf". This can be done with the 'profile' configuration command. Note: if IP tunnels are required, it is necessary to change out of the DC-Optimized profile.

description: Symptom: A VSF stack member switch unexpectedly reboots due to the ops-switchd process crashing with a signal: 6 error. Scenario: In some scenarios with excessive ACL event logging, a crash may occur due to a failure in the system when processing a high volume of ACL log entries. Workaround: Minimize the use of the log keyword in ACL entries, especially on high-traffic rules. Consider using ACL counters instead of logging for monitoring traffic patterns.

description: Symptom: In an L2VPN network, when a DHCP client sends an IP request with the broadcast flag unset (unicast), duplicate DHCP client packets are observed at the DHCP server-one from each VTEP. As a result, the client does not receive an IP address. Scenario: To prevent duplicate DHCP client packets from each VTEP in an L2VPN topology, it is recommended to configure "no dhcp-relay l2vpn-clients" on the switch. However, after saving the switch configuration and rebooting, the relay daemon does not correctly apply all configuration settings at boot time, even though the configuration persists in the database. This causes the duplicate packet issue to reappear. Workaround: To restore correct behavior, manually enable and then disable the L2VPN client configuration using "dhcp-relay l2vpn-clients" followed by "no dhcp-relay l2vpn-clients".

symptom: In an L2VPN network, when a DHCP client sends an IP request with the broadcast flag unset (unicast), duplicate DHCP client packets are observed at the DHCP server-one from each VTEP. As a result, the client does not receive an IP address.
scenario: To prevent duplicate DHCP client packets from each VTEP in an L2VPN topology, it is recommended to configure "no dhcp-relay l2vpn-clients" on the switch. However, after saving the switch configuration and rebooting, the relay daemon does not correctly apply all configuration settings at boot time, even though the configuration persists in the database. This causes the duplicate packet issue to reappear.
workaround: To restore correct behavior, manually enable and then disable the L2VPN client configuration using "dhcp-relay l2vpn-clients" followed by "no dhcp-relay l2vpn-clients".
